article_meta.title: The three-way battle between Democrats running for Senate in Illinois has evolved into an argument about the best way to

reasoning: The summary uses neutral, factual language, avoids the article’s rhetorical flourishes, and attributes positions appropriately. It does not adopt loaded framing or assert opinions as facts. Minor issue: sources_represented lists only the outlet, not the perspectives, but this doesn’t reflect framing inheritance.

custom_reasoning.unsubstantiated_claims: Claims reflect the article and are either attributed or framed cautiously (e.g., “some Democrats fear”).
custom_reasoning.opinion_as_fact: No subjective judgments stated as fact; evaluative points are attributed.
custom_reasoning.sensationalism: No dramatic or alarming language introduced.
custom_reasoning.mind_reading: No assertions about motives or intentions without attribution.
custom_reasoning.slant: Perspectives of all three candidates and intra-party concerns are presented proportionally.
custom_reasoning.bias_by_omission: Major perspectives in the article (varying Democratic stances and political risk concerns) are included.
custom_reasoning.word_choice: Politically neutral wording; mirrors article terms in a non-loaded way.
custom_reasoning.negativity_bias: Balanced treatment; no overemphasis on negative outcomes.
custom_reasoning.elite_populist_bias: No preferential treatment of elites or grassroots; focus is on candidates’ positions.
custom_reasoning.framing_neutrality: Language is more neutral than any implicit framing in the source; avoids loaded phrasing.
custom_reasoning.perspective_completeness: The summary covers perspectives, but sources_represented fails to list distinct viewpoints (candidates, intra-party factions), listing only the outlet.
